title: Wesley Vandesteene is 2026's real underdog rise
meta: Published: 17 March 2026 | F1CC News Feature Desk | Elena Vasquez
caption: Lead image: Wesley Vandesteene in the Mercedes garage.
body:
The spray at Albert Park hung low, a silver mist that blurred the line between track and sky, and through it Wesley Vandesteene carved a path that felt less like a race and more like a slow, deliberate exhale. If you want a true underdog arc in 2026, start with him, the man who emerged from that spray as if he'd been there all along. His 2025 profile was modest: 21st in the standings with 32 points. Last year he was a leaf on a midfield stream, drifting where the current took him. Useful pace, limited return, and little reason to expect he would be one of the names shaping the top of the table this early in the next season. That was then.

Two rounds into 2026, the picture has changed as swiftly as a summer storm. Vandesteene's second place in Australia was no fluke, no lucky break in a wet-weather lottery. It was a composed, searing drive through conditions that turned the track into a river, delivering the silver arrow one of the most valuable single-race results of the early year. Even with a difficult China weekend, where his car was a shadow of its Melbourne self, he sits on 18 points and remains in the front section of the standings, still clinging to the light.

The key shift is not just points. It is the quality of the light he now reflects. In 2025, Vandesteene was a driver fighting for survival, barely visible in a crowded midfield cloud. In 2026, he has become a driver who can seize a moment when the front-runners blink, a sudden shaft of sunlight through a gap in the grey. That is a very different skill set. It turns a fringe name into a season-long factor, a name the weather will not wash away.

He is not the title favourite, and no one pretends otherwise. But that is precisely why this underdog story shimmers. From 32 points and 21st in the gloom of last year to a podium and early relevance, Vandesteene has already done enough to change the paddock's conversation, to make them look twice at the spray. The next step is proving that this is the new baseline, that he can race not just when the heavens open but when the sun beats down and the track is bone dry. The storm has passed; now he must learn to dance in the light.
